name: observability-monitoring-slo-implement
description: You are an SLO (Service Level Objective) expert specializing in implementing reliability standards and error budget-based practices. Design SLO frameworks, define SLIs, and build monitoring that balances reliability with delivery velocity.
capabilities: Design SLO frameworks with appropriate availability targets for different service tiers
Define and implement SLIs for availability, latency, throughput, and error rates
Create error budget policies and burn rate alerting strategies
Build Prometheus monitoring queries and recording rules for SLO tracking
Establish SLO governance processes and stakeholder communication templates
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis detected 57 potential issues, but manual review confirms all findings are false positives. The skill contains documentation with Python code examples for SLO implementation - no actual executable code, no network calls, and no cryptographic operations. The placeholder URLs use example.com domain. This is a legitimate DevOps reliability skill.
valueStatement: Design and implement Service Level Objectives with SLIs and error budgets to measure and improve system reliability while balancing feature velocity.
limitations: Does not implement actual monitoring infrastructure - only provides guidance and templates
Requires access to service telemetry and metrics data to be effective
Does not cover incident response automation beyond alerting
Templates require customization for specific service architectures

useCases.title: Define SLOs for a new API service
useCases.description: Create availability, latency, and error rate SLOs with appropriate targets based on service criticality

useCases.title: Set up error budget alerting
useCases.description: Configure multi-window burn rate alerts to detect fast and slow error budget consumption

useCases.title: Establish SLO review process
useCases.description: Create weekly SLO review templates and governance processes for engineering teams

promptTemplates.title: Basic SLO Design
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me design SLOs for my payment processing service. It handles 10,000 requests per minute and requires high reliability. What availability target should I set and how do I define the SLIs?
promptTemplates.scenario: Starting from scratch with a new critical service

promptTemplates.title: SLI Implementation
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to implement SLIs for a REST API service using Prometheus. Show me how to create availability and latency SLI queries that track the percentage of successful requests and requests under 500ms.

promptTemplates.title: Error Budget Alerts
promptTemplates.prompt: Configure error budget burn rate alerts for my service with a 99.9% SLO target. I need both fast burn (page immediately) and slow burn (create ticket) alert rules.
promptTemplates.scenario: Setting up alerting for budget consumption

promptTemplates.title: SLO Governance
promptTemplates.description: Create a weekly SLO review process template that includes performance review, incident review, and decision-making sections for release approvals.
promptTemplates.prompt: Establish an SLO governance framework for my team with roles and responsibilities, weekly review templates, and stakeholder communication processes.
promptTemplates.scenario: Setting up team processes and governance

bestPractices: Start with conservative SLO targets and tighten them based on actual service performance data
Use multiple time windows for burn rate alerts to catch both fast and slow budget consumption
Align SLO targets with business priorities and user expectations, not technical convenience
antiPatterns: Setting SLO targets too tight initially, leading to constant alerts and alert fatigue
Using only availability SLIs without considering latency or quality metrics
Creating SLOs without stakeholder alignment or business context

faq.question: What is the difference between an SLO and an SLA?
faq.answer: An SLO (Service Level Objective) is an internal target that engineering teams commit to. An SLA (Service Level Agreement) is a contractual commitment to customers with financial consequences if violated.

faq.question: How do I choose the right SLO availability target?
faq.answer: Start by analyzing historical availability, understanding user expectations, and considering business impact. Critical services typically need 99.95%+ while standard services may target 99.5%.

faq.question: What time window should I use for SLO measurements?
faq.answer: Common windows are 30 days for rolling availability or calendar months for billing periods. Longer windows provide stability but slower feedback on issues.

faq.question: How do I handle scheduled maintenance in SLO calculations?
faq.answer: Exclude planned maintenance windows from SLO measurements or use availability formulas that account for expected downtime. Document your approach clearly.

faq.question: What should I do when error budget is depleted?
faq.answer: Pause feature development, focus on reliability improvements, and communicate status to stakeholders. Use the error budget policy to guide release decisions.

faq.question: How many SLOs should a service have?
faq.answer: Start with 2-4 SLOs covering the most important user-facing aspects: availability, latency, and error rate. Add more as needed but avoid alert fatigue.
